Fern Por-rrrcAt- Pnecrrcgs C oMMrssroN
-128 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacranrento. CA 95g14-2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322_0886
September 8, 2008
Roy W. Wasden
Chief of Police
City of Modesto
600 -lOth Street
Modesto, CA95353-1746
Re
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-08-t3S
Dear Mr. Wasden:
This letter responds to your reqllest for advice regarding the gift provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the ..Act'.).r 
.
QUESTTON
May you receive payments for travel and other expenses related to yoLrr
participation in panel presentations for the Hawaii state Law Enforcement officials
Association conference?
CONCLUSION
You may receive gifts of travel that are directly connected to your participation as
a panelist and attendee of the law enforcement confelence. payments ro, you. ,i,
transportation are reportable on your statement of economic interests (..s8I,,). payments
for nec'es.:;ur1' lodging,and meals directly related to your speech and panel or" no,
reportable on your sEI or subject to gift limits. However, you may i,cur reporting
obligations for gifis that are nol tlecessary and not directly connected to your delivering a
speech or participating in a panel at the Association's convention if the value of the gifts is
$50 or more from a single sottrce, and that source is required to be report.Jon you. srr.
I rhe Political Retirrm Act is contained in Goventment Code Seetions lll{x)0 through gl(}1.1. All
stiltutory ret'erences are kl the Gov'g111n]"r, code. unless otherwise indicated. The re_uulations tlf the Fair
Political Practices Cilmmission are contained in Scr;tions llil l0 thnrugt, rsgcz of ritle 2 of the califilrnia
code of Regulatittns. ,\ll regulatrlry ret'erenses are to Title 2. Divisioi 6 of the culifirrnia Code of
Regulations. unless otherwise indicated_


File No. A-08-135
Page No. 2
In addition, these gifts may subject yolr to disqualification under the Act's conflict-of-
interest rules.
FACTS
ANALYSIS
You are the chief of police for the City of Modesto. You have been invited and
agreed to present information and participate in panel presentations in Hawaii. The
presentations are to the Hawaii State Law Enforiemeni Offi.iolr Association
("Association"), a 501(cX3) non-profit grolrp, and the Association will be paying for your
travel and other expenses. In our telephone conversation of Augus t 26, ZOOg',you said
that other expenses covered will include your airport parking, meals, hotel costs, and
incidentals. The Association will be hosting all the lunches, dinners, and hospitality suite
events' You are not receiving any payment for your participation in the conflrence.
You will travel to Hawaii on Tuesday, September 23,2o}B,and will present on
Wednesday, Septembet 24 andThursday, September 25. You will attend the conference
on Friday, September 26 and, will return to California on Saturday, September 27.
You ask whether you may accept these travel payments consistent with the
provisions of the Act.
Receipt of Gifts
The Act imposes different obligations on public officials regarding the receipt of
gifts.
1' Gift Reporting.- The Act requires public officials to report the receipt of certain
gifts on their statement of economic interests. when an official is an elected state or local
officer, or in any position specified under Section 872oo,he or she must report 4ll gifts of
$50 or more from r-:,14" source during the applicable reporting period. (Sections g7200,
87202-87205, and 87207.) Additionally, an agency's .onhi.t-oi-interest code may require
a designated employee to report gifts from rp""ifi.O persons on the official,s SEL Gifts
from each reportable source must be t"g9rya when tirey amount to $50 or more dr"rring the
applicable reporting period. (sections g7 3oz(b) and g7 zo7 (a)( l ).)
2' Gift Limit. Section 89503 imposes a gift limit on persons designated under
\7299 and on designated employees of state and local government agencies. Section
89503(b) prohibits public officials specified under Seclion 87zoo froln accepting gifts
totaling more than $390 in value from any single source in any calendar yeai. Section
89503(c) also provides that no designated employee of a state or local government agency
may accept gifts from any single source in any calendar year in excess'of the gift limit if
the official is required to report the receipt of gifts from ihat sollrce on his or her statement


File No. A-08-135
Page No. 3
of economic interests. The cLlrrent gift limit is $390, but this amount is adjusted each odd-
numbered year to reflect changes in the Consumer Price Index. (Section S"9SO:19;
Regulation 18940.2.)
3. Conflicts of Interest. When an official receives gifts aggregating $390 more
from the same soLlrce within the l2 months prior to when the official-parti"cipates in a
govemmental decision that has a financial impact on the solrrce, the fficial may have a
conflict of interest under rhe Act. (Sections 97100 and g7103(e).)
Because you are the police chief, a designated employee of the city of Modesto, a
local government agency. we assume that you have full disciosure under your city's
conflict-of-interest code.' Therefore, gifts from the Association are reportable on your
SEI, and the above provisions apply to you.
Gifts of Travel
Section 82028(a) defines a .,gift,' as:
"[A]ny payment that confers a personal benefit on the recipient, to the
extent that consideration ofequal or greater value is not received and
includes a rebate or discount in the piice of anything of value unless the
rebate or discount is made in the regular collrse of business to members of
the public without regard to official status. ..." (Emphasis added.)
You stated that your purpose in making the trip is to deliver a speech and
participate in a panel at the Association's 2008 conferince in Hawaii. Any travel is
presumed to confer some personal benefit on a public official.3
Gifts of Travel --Exceptions
Under some circumstances, payments for transportation, Iodging, and subsistence
may be exempt from the definition of "gift." In others, they may be ref,ortable gifts but
exempt from the gift limit.
Section 89506(a), in relevant part, provides an exception from the gift limits for
certain travel payments:
: If your agency's cont-lict-of'-interest code has narrow disclosure requirements fbr your position.
you may want to ask us fbr turther advice.
I In limited situations, payments fbr travel may constitute "income" to an oftlsial if the ofTicial
provides equal tlr greater consideration in return. However, we have concluded that merely giving a speech
does not constitute providing equal or greater consideration for travel payments. (Robet Advice Letter. No.
,4-00-266: Allen Advice Letrer. No. A-96- | g L ) you have nor ,tltea tirai you will pruuid" any irther
consideration besides giving a speech and participating in a panel. Accordingty. we characteiize the travel
payments you will receive as gitis. unless an exception applies.


File No. A-08-135
Page No. 4
"(a) Payments, advances, or reimbursements, for travel, including actLlal
transportation and related lodging and subsistence which is reasonably
related to a legislative or govemmental purpose, or to an issue of state,
national, or international public policy, are not prohibited or limited by
this chapter if either of the following apply:
"(l) The travel is in connection with a speech given by the elected state
officer, local elected officeholder, candidate for elected state office or
local elected office, an individual specified in Section g7200, member of
a state board or commission, or designated employee of a state or local
government agency, the lodging and subsistence expenses are
limited to the day immediarely preceding, the day of,
and the day immediarely following the speech, and the
travel is within the United States.
"(2) The travel is provided by a government, a govemmental agency, a
foreign government, a governmental authority, i bonu fide pubiic oi
private educational institution, as defined in Section 203 of the Revenue
and Taxation Code, a nonprofit charitable or religious organization which
is exempt from taxation under Section 501(cX3) of the Internal Revenue
code, or by a person domiciled outside the United states which
substantially satisfies the requirements for tax-exempt status under
Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code..'
In addition, Regulation 18950.3 provides a limited exemption from both the gift
limits and gift reporting requirements for admission, refreshments, and similar non-cash
nominal benefits on the day of a speech, as well as any necessary lodging and subsistence
provided directly in connection with a speech or similar activity.4
Regulation I 8950.3 provides:
"Free admission, and refreshments and similar non-cash nominal benefits provided
to a filer during the entire event at which the filer gives a speech, participates in a
panel or seminar, or provides a similar service . . . and any-necessary lod^ging and
a Speech Given: Pursuant to Regulation 1893 l.l, "speech given" means a public address, oration. or other
fbrm of oral presentation' and includes participation in a pa-nel, seminar, or debate. we have advised that an
appearance on a television show to participate in a panel discussion qualifies as a "speech given" by a public
official' (Manson Advice Letter, No. 4-94-308.) However. merely being a "discussant" at a conf-erence
does not quality as giving a speech or oral presentation as required by the regulati on. (Hoeltn Advice Letter,
I-92-392.) Similarly. holding informal meetings with constitu"nt, oio few individuals who are members of
a particular group would not qualify. (Williams Advice Letter, No. I-95-405.) In addition. infbrmal remarks
made at an event do not c'onstitute "giving a speech" fbr purposes of Regulation l g93 l.l and Section
89506(a)(l). (Fuller Advice Leter. No. 4-02-307.)


File No. A-08-135
Page No.5
sttbsistence provided directly in connection with the speech, panel, seminar, or
service, including but not limited to meals and beverages on it " 
doy of the activity,
are not payments and need not be reported by any filer.,'
we next analyze whether these exceptions apply to your travel.
Payments Provided in connection to a speech, panel, or seminar
Necessary Lodging and Meals: An official's necessctry lodging and "subsistence,,,
including meals and beverages, provided directly in connection *ith an event at which the
official giv:s- 1 speech or participates in a panel or seminar are not reportable and are not
subject to gift limits. (Regulation 18950.3.) This exceprion for nrr"irr,ry lodging and
subsistence provided directly in connection with o rp"""h, panel, or seminar, ipplies to all
locations, even outside the United States.
Please note, however, that what constitutes "necessary accommodations,, within
this exception is generally limited to the day of the speech, and the day before and after f
necessctry due to travel arrangements. Any accommodations outside tlese parameters
may be considered a gift subject to normal reporting rules and gift limits. (,UbnAdvice
Letter, No.I-94-270: Nishite Advice Letter, No. A:91_56g.)
You are traveling to Hawaii on Tuesday. Septembe r 23,2008,and will be arriving
th-ere the evening prior to your scheduled speech. You will speak at the convention the
afternoon of Wednesday, September 24, and will participate in panels on Thursday,
September 25, ending around noon that day. You-also indicateihot you will attend the
remainder of the convention on Friday, September 26,2008, and you are not scheduled to
make any speeches-or participate in any panel discussions that day. you fly back to
California on the afternoon of Saturday, September 27,200g.
Accordingly, payments for admission, and refreshments and similar non-cash
nominal benefits as well as necessary lodging and meals you receive in connection with
speeches, panels, and seminars in Hawali are not subject io gift limits and. are not
reportable on your statement of economic interests.
The exclusion for necessary lodging, meals, and beverages under this regulation is
generally limited to those provided on the day of your speech aid participation in the
panel discttssions. However, because the travel is from Californiaio Hawaii, we consider
payments for lodging, meals, and beverages, the day prior to your speech as necessary
travel expenses, and payments for these items ur. ,oir.po.tobl" or iubject to gift limits.
These include, for instance, yollr hotel accommodations on: the evening of
wednesday, September 23,2008, the day before your speech; Thursday, septe"mber 24__
the same day which you make a speech on the Laci peterson case; and rhur.sday,


File No. A-08-135
Page No.6
September 25, 2008-the day yoll participate in a panel discussion about law enforcement
and the media in major cases.
Sinrilarly, exempt from reporting or gift limits are the refreshments, meals, and
nominal benefits you receive while: traveling to Hawaii on September 23,200g; and in
connection with the September 24,2008 speech and the September 25, Z11gpanel (such
as the continental breakfasts, luncheon events, and the hospitality suite events for
presenters and participants hosted by the Association).
Payments for lodging, meals, beverages and incidentals the tlay after your
participation in panels (Friday, September 27,2008), would not be .onriO"r"a necessary
travel payments. Therefore, they would be reportable gifts, not subject to gift limits.
(Allen Advice Letter, supra; Nishite Advice Lerter, ,uprn; Section SqSOOf;)tf l.l
Transportation inside the U.S.: The exceptions described above apply to a gift of
transportation in the United States if connected to delivering a speech, po*i or similar
service. However, transportation to and from the event is not reportable and not subject
to gtft limits under Regulation 18950.3 only if travel rs within California. Section g9506
provides that transportation to and from an event like a rp.""fr+u*f, * seminar is a
reportable gift, but not subject to gift limits, if the travel is within the United s,"i* -
Because the travel payments will be for airfare outside california, but within the
United States, the payments for your airfare to Hawaii are reportable on your SEI, but not
subject to gift limits. (Section 89506; Regulation 1g950.3.)
Other payments
Travel payments that do not fall under the exceptions described above are
reportable gifts subject to gift limits. Any payments not directly related to your speech or
panel presentation at the Association's 2008 convention that aggregate to $50 or more
from a single source would be considered gifts subject to the A&'r1"po.ting rules and gift
limits, and disqualification under the Act'Jconfliciof interest provisions.
Conflict of Interest Disquaffication
ln addition, a public official who receives gift(s) of $390 or more may have a
financial conflict of interest under the Act. (Sections 87100 et seq.) The official must
disqualify himself or herself from voting or otherwise participating in a govemmental
decision affecting that source, if the payment was received or promised to the official
within 12 months preceding the decision. Therefore, if you reieive a gift va1-red at $390
or more from a source reportable on your SEI, y_ou *oyb. prohibitedlrom participating in
governmental decisions affecting that source. If this o..r-,., you should seek further
assistance.


File No. A-08-135
Page No.7
Honoraia
Section 89502(a) states that "no elected state officer, elected officer of a local
government agency, or other individual specified in Section 87200 shall accept any
honorarium." section 8950r (a) defines honorarium as ..any payment made in
consideration for any speech given, article published, or attindance at any public or
private conference, convention, meeting, social event, meal, or like gathering."
You stated in your request for advice that you will not be paid and will not be
accepting any honoraria for speeches or similar events.
Additionally, Regulation 18932.4(e) states that "honorarium" does not include
"any necessary lodging and subsistence provided directly in connection" with an event in
which an official gives a speech or participates in a pun"i o. seminar. Accordingly,
payment of actual and necessary lodging and subsistence to in connection with your
speech and participation in panels and seminars are not prohibited by the provisions of the
Act banning honoraria.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
,Z
By: Emelyn Rodriguez
Counsel, Legal Division
ERjgl

Section 89502(a) states that "no elected state officer, elected officer of a local
government agency, or other individual specified in Section 87200 shall accept any
honorarium." section 8950r (a) defines honorarium as ..any payment made in
consideration for any speech given, article published, or attindance at any public or
private conference, convention, meeting, social event, meal, or like gathering."
You stated in your request for advice that you will not be paid and will not be
accepting any honoraria for speeches or similar events.
Additionally, Regulation 18932.4(e) states that "honorarium" does not include
"any necessary lodging and subsistence provided directly in connection" with an event in
which an official gives a speech or participates in a pun"i o. seminar. Accordingly,
payment of actual and necessary lodging and subsistence to in connection with your
speech and participation in panels and seminars are not prohibited by the provisions of the
Act banning honoraria.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

FACTS ANALYSIS You are the chief of police for the City of Modesto. You have been invited and agreed to present information and participate in panel presentations in Hawaii. The presentations are to the Hawaii State Law Enforiemeni Offi.iolr Association ("Association"), a 501(cX3) non-profit grolrp, and the Association will be paying for your travel and other expenses. In our telephone conversation of Augus t 26, ZOOg',you said that other expenses covered will include your airport parking, meals, hotel costs, and incidentals. The Association will be hosting all the lunches, dinners, and hospitality suite events' You are not receiving any payment for your participation in the conflrence. You will travel to Hawaii on Tuesday, September 23,2o}B,and will present on Wednesday, Septembet 24 andThursday, September 25. You will attend the conference on Friday, September 26 and, will return to California on Saturday, September 27. You ask whether you may accept these travel payments consistent with the provisions of the Act. Receipt of Gifts The Act imposes different obligations on public officials regarding the receipt of gifts. 1' Gift Reporting.- The Act requires public officials to report the receipt of certain gifts on their statement of economic interests. when an official is an
